title: Quantum anomalous Hall effect in chiral semimetals
authors: Peng-Yi Liu, Yu-Hao Wan, Qing-Feng Sun
abstract: The quantum anomalous Hall (QAH) effect is conventionally understood to exist only in Chern insulators, while a recent study has shown that ferromagnetic metals can also host the QAH effect. Between insulators and metals, we demonstrate that QAH can persist even in a chiral semimetal, where conduction and valence bands touch at zero energy. Transport calculations demonstrate that the Hall conductivity of such a system can be quantized in the presence of dephasing. Interestingly, its longitudinal conductivity remains finite and exhibits semimetallic behavior, in contrast to Chern insulators. This unusual transport behavior originates from the quantization of the Berry curvature integral over occupied states and the semimetallic band structure. This chiral semimetal can transition into a Chern insulator, accompanied by the vanishing of longitudinal conductivity and a reduction of the intrinsic length scale of the Hall response. Our results extend the concept of QAH and uncover the semimetallic QAH transport signatures.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the Quantum Anomalous Hall effect is not restricted to insulators and can exist in chiral semimetals. It shows that while the Hall response remains quantized, the longitudinal conductivity stays finite, creating a unique 'dual-channel' transport regime. This discovery extends the fundamental understanding of topological transport in gapless systems.
main_problem: Investigating whether the Quantum Anomalous Hall (QAH) effect can persist in chiral semimetals where conduction and valence bands touch at zero energy, and identifying its unique transport signatures.
main_result: The Hall conductivity remains quantized even in the semimetallic regime due to the cancellation of Berry curvature at specific valleys, while the longitudinal conductivity remains finite and exhibits semimetallic behavior.
method: Nonequilibrium Green's function (NEGF) calculations using a six-terminal Hall bar geometry and Büttiker virtual leads to simulate dephasing.
model_or_system: A 2D chiral semimetal described by a two-band tight-binding model on a square lattice, featuring massive Dirac fermions at Gamma and M points and gapless cones at X and Y points.
key_observables: Hall conductivity (sigma_xy), longitudinal conductivity (sigma_xx), Hall resistivity (rho_xy), and equilibrium current density (Jy).
important_parameters: Fermi energy (Ef), dephasing strength (Gamma_d), mass term (m), and device dimensions (Lx, Ly).
main_assumptions: The system is modeled using a clean tight-binding Hamiltonian with dephasing introduced via virtual leads to simulate realistic experimental conditions.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the 2D band structure and nanoribbon spatial distribution; Fig 2 displays the Hall bar setup and transport results (sigma_xx, sigma_xy vs Ef); Fig 4 shows the Hall response and current decay; Fig 5 analyzes the dependence of conductivities on dephasing, device size, and probe distance.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of QAH in semimetals, presents the tight-binding model and band structure, details the NEGF transport simulation setup, analyzes the quantization and semimetallic signatures, explores the semimetal-to-insulator transition, and discusses the role of dephasing and device geometry.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Two-dimensional band structure of the chiral semimetal model. (b) Band structure of a chiral semimetal nanoribbon, with the color encoding the position expectation of each state ⟨x⟩/Lx This nanoribbon has a width in the x- direction Lx = 100 and is of infinite extent in the y-direction. Throughout this paper, we use A = B = 1.

FIG. 2. (a) Schematic of a six-terminal Hall bar. Six leads are shown in orange, and the center region (shown in blue) is made of chiral semimetals. The width of lead-L and R is Lx = 100a, the width of Lead-(1-4) is Ly = 50a, and the spacing between the leads is L0 = 50a. (b) Longitudinal σT xx and Hall σT xy conductivity vs. Fermi energy EF , in the unit of e2/h, calculated by the transport simulation. We use a dephasing strength Γd = 0.5. The dashed line shows the quantum conductivity. (c) and (d) respectively show the σT xx and σT xy vs. Γd, in the unit of e2/h, with three different EF .

FIG. 3. (a) Berry curvature Ωdistribution of the lower band of Eq. (2) in the Brillouin zone. (b) Band structure plotted along high-symmetry lines, with the Berry curvature encoded by the color scale, using the same color bar as in panel (a). (c) Linear-response Hall conductivity σL xy, obtained by integrating the Berry curvature of all occupied states below the Fermi energy, as a function of the Fermi energy, in units of e2/h.

FIG. 4. (a) Band structure of the insulating QAH nanorib- bon with the mass m = 1. This nanoribbon has a width in the x-direction Lx = 100 and is of infinite extent in the y-direction. (b) The longitudinal σT xx and Hall σT xy conduc- tivities calculated by the transport simulations, with the unit of e2/h and Γd = 0. In (a) and (b), m = 1. (c) Linear- response Hall conductivity σL xy for different m, obtained by integrating the Berry curvature of all occupied states below the Fermi energy EF , as a function of the Fermi energy, in units of e2/h.
